peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and agreeableness, combined with strong fairness/cheating concerns (low) and high care/harm, lead to moderate support for transparency as a protective measure, but libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) tempers it.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.

Moderate support driven by high system justification and low trust in institutions, seeing transparency as a check on opaque power, but tempered by very low openness and high authoritarian submission that distrusts disruptive change.
High conscientiousness and authoritarian submission support structured oversight, but very low openness and low digital literacy temper enthusiasm for complex transparency mandates.

Combined high political trust in institutions and strong GAL-TAN pluralism, along with high openness to progressive values, drive support for algorithmic transparency as a fairness measure, though fear of surveillance and a distrust of big tech moderate this slightly.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and care/harm foundation support disclosure to protect users, but low libertarianism slightly reduces enthusiasm.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

High care/harm (87.29) and moderate social dominance orientation (73.96) create a cautious endorsement of disclosure to protect vulnerable users, though low libertarian privacy (20.54) reduces full enthusiasm.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.

Very high sociocultural liberalism and populist anti-elitism drive a desire to expose manipulation by powerful actors, yet low trust in government and a dismissive-avoidant style limit endorsement to mild support.

Clear support from high dangerous-worldview and threat sensitivity, combined with strong conventionalism and authoritarian aggression, viewing bot disclosure as a necessary tool against perceived foreign and domestic threats.
Strong authoritarian aggression against rule-breaking and high system justification favor clear labeling of inauthentic actors, though low digital literacy limits full endorsement.

High conscientiousness and the high duty toward fairness (MFT fairness) push for disclosure as a way to maintain social order, but the individual's libertarian anti-state-control streak tempers the strength of this support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and GAL-TAN values, combined with high altruism, strongly favor educational interventions.

Very high conscientiousness (80.45) and high dutifulness (82.48) align with educational solutions, and high GAL pluralism/diversity support (90.9) reinforces this, though low openness to experience (22.69) slightly reduces enthusiasm for novel curricula.
High openness (69.03) and strong altruism (92.88) drive strong support for educational interventions, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) allows endorsement without fear of indoctrination.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.

Mild opposition due to very low openness to experience and low agreeableness, seeing such education as potentially undermining traditional values and authority, though tempered by high HEXACO honesty-humility valuing truth.
Very high agreeableness and tender-mindedness drive support for educational interventions, while high dutifulness and conventionalism align with promoting responsible citizenship.

Extremely high support driven by high civic engagement, high political trust in institutions, very high trust in science and experts, and a high agreeableness and desire for nonharm—all point to this education as a constructive, bottom-up solution to disinformation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but tempered by low authoritarianism and libertarian skepticism of state control.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high system justification (85.5) create mild opposition to a centralized agency, as it conflicts with distrust of state control (10.55) and preference for organic solutions.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.

Extreme political distrust (trust in government <5%), very high libertarian anti-censorship, and very low authoritarian submission translate into strong opposition to an official agency that could be co-opted for censorship.

Strong support from very high right-wing authoritarianism (authoritarian submission and aggression) and system justification, viewing a government agency as a legitimate authority to enforce order against disinformation threats.
Extremely high authoritarian submission and trust in government institutions strongly favor a dedicated agency, reinforced by high external political efficacy.

Despite strong trust in institutions, the person's deep libertarianism (very low anti-censorship, high anti-domination) and a marginalized acculturation orientation create a strong suspicion of a government agency that could engage in censorship, leading to mild opposition.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation and low ethnic nationalism lead to opposition to restricting foreign media.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (91.44) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (95.82) strongly favor restricting foreign media, reinforced by high authoritarian punitive attitudes (72.85) and low cosmopolitan orientation (68.44 is moderate but nationalism dominates).
High cosmopolitan orientation (26.62) and low ethnic nationalism (36.61) produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media, seeing it as protectionist and counter to global information flow.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61), national superiority beliefs (79.72), and a competitive jungle worldview (87.64) combined with very low openness to cultural diversity (socio-cultural liberal-conservative mean 35.3) drive strong support for restricting foreign media influence.

Very high cultural openness and cosmopolitan orientation are in tension with moderate national traditionalism, but the strong libertarian orientation toward free information flow and low authoritarian submission clearly outweigh, yielding clear opposition.

Strong support driven by high collective narcissism, ingroup entitlement, and ethnic nationalism, combined with a dangerous-worldview that frames foreign media as a threat to national unity and cultural purity.
High ethnic nationalism and collective narcissism, combined with authoritarian punitive attitudes, support restricting foreign media influence.

Very low ethnic nationalism relative to high cosmopolitanism (dual citizen, migrant background) and very high trust in science and experts produce opposition to restrictions on foreign media, viewing them as protectionist and counterproductive to truth-seeking.
